Themes & Innovation Since 1983
WHAT
I LEARNED
THIS
WEEK®
Believe me, every heart
has its secret sorrow which
the world knows not; and
oftentimes we call a man
cold, when he is only sad.
Henry Wadsworth
Longfellow
It is essential to understand
that battles are primarily won
in the hearts of men. Men
respond to leadership in a
most remarkable way and
once you have won his heart,
he will follow you anywhere.
Vince Lombardi
A nation that forgets its past
has no future.
Winston Churchill
I murmured to Picasso
that I liked his portrait of
Gertrude Stein. Yes, he
said, everybody says that
she does not look like it but
that does not make any
difference, she will.
The Autobiography of
Alice B. Toklas
What is hell? I maintain
that it is the suffering of
not being able to love.
Fyodor Dostoyevsky
I said to the man who stood at
the gate of the year "Give me
a light that I may tread safely
into the unknown." And he
replied, "Go into the darkness
and put your hand into the
hand of God. That shall be to
you better than light and safer
than a known way!"
King George VI in his 1939
Christmas message
broadcast on eve of WWII
Death is not the greatest loss
in life. The greatest loss is
what dies inside while we live.
Norman Cousins
To succeed in the world, it
is much more necessary to
possess the penetration to
discern who is a fool, than
to discover who is a clever
man.
Talleyrand
We think too small, like
the frog at the bottom of
the well. He thinks the sky
is only as big as the top
of the well. If he surfaced,
he would have an entirely
different view.
Mao Tse-Tung
A great man is one who seizes
the vital issue in a complex
question, what we might call
the jugular vein of the whole
organism, and spends his
energies upon that.
Joseph Rickaby
The masses have never
thirsted after truth. They
turn aside from evidence
that is not to their taste,
preferring to deify error,
if error seduces them.
Whoever can supply them
with illusions is easily their
master; whoever attempts
to destroy their illusions is
always their victim.
Gustave Le Bon (1841-
1931), The Crowd

WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
STRATEGY & ASSET ALLOCATION &
PERFORMANCE OF HIGH CONVICTION IDEAS
Since September 2020, we have strongly and repeatedly argued that a classic
paradigm shift into the new market leaders was starting, which would feature
commodities, commodity-related and inflation-sensitive sectors, and many of the
long-depressed markets.
In October 2020, with UST 10-year yields at 0.78%, we warned that bond yields would
rise significantly and for a long time as the 40-year bull market in bonds transitions to a
secular bear market.
For the past 3-4 years, the great question among Western investors has been: “Is
China uninvestable?” As long-term contrarians, that is music to our ears! We believe
Chinese stocks are in the early stages of a secular bull market.
Other important recent developments that support the basis for our high conviction
themes:
 The ratio of gold to the U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI) broke-out in September
2024 from a 45-year downtrend-line.
 The outperformance of the gold miners versus the major U.S. stock market indices
has broken-out, as suggested by rising bond-market inflation expectations.
 Sensitive inflation indicators in the markets are starting to come alive.
 The U.S. Dollar appears to be entering a long-term downtrend, which is bullish for
commodities and inflation.
Silver has outperformed gold. And the relative-performance of the silver miners
has broken-out against almost every broad stock-market index in the world.
Agricultural commodities and related-stocks are starting to rise, as several agriculture
ETFs and fertilizer stocks have broken-out.
These developments lead to the following conclusions:
1. Gold is set to outperform the S&P 500 and international stock-markets for many years to come.
2. The shift toward hard assets is likely to gain further momentum.
3. China’s stock-market could be among the best-performing equity markets in the world.
The Highest Conviction ideas reflect this view, with the following major
allocations:

As we go to press, the ceasefire agreed to on Tuesday evening Eastern time is
under growing strain. The U.S. continues to send military assets to the region,
the most visible being the USS Bush Carrier Strike Group. And on Wednesday
evening, the speaker of the Iranian Parliament, and one of Tehran’s most senior
officials, tweeted:
3 clauses [of Iran’s 10-Point Proposal that Trump said would form a workable
basis on which to negotiate] have been violated…in such a situation a
bilateral ceasefire or negotiations is unreasonable.
However, regardless of whether the ceasefire holds, there is a deeper and far
more consequential development that could reshape the regional balance of
power in the Middle East and by extension have broader global implications.
Six weeks of war appears to have re-prioritized and elevated the Strait
of Hormuz in Tehran’s geostrategic calculus. Tehran now views Hormuz
as critical to restoring its deterrence, to ensuring its national security and to
reshaping the regional post-war order in its favor. This is a big shift from six
weeks ago when the regime’s nuclear programme, network of regional proxies
WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
and ballistic missile programme were its primary modes of deterrence. From the
regime’s perspective, this hybrid approach failed to provide sufficient deterrence
to prevent the U.S. and Israel from assassinating the Supreme Leader and
initiating six weeks of strikes on military and civilian infrastructure.
A new and evolving deterrent strategy now appears to be in the works whereby
Tehran is looking to place heavier emphasis on economic deterrence going
forwards alongside its current military-deterrence capabilities. It is within
this context that the regime is starting to leverage what it describes as “an
imposed war”
( \cos \frac { \cos } { \cdot } \sin \alpha \cos \beta \cos \alpha )
by American and Israeli aggressors to fundamentally
reframe the role of the Strait of Hormuz in its geostrategic calculus such that
Tehran emerges as an integral and elevated actor in the post-war regional
architecture.
The last six weeks have underlined to Tehran how its most powerful weapon is
not so much its missile and drone capabilities per se but rather its ability to de
facto control the flow of vessels through the Strait of Hormuz and by extension
the leverage this creates on global energy markets, insurance rates and maritime
security.
This evolution in Tehran’s deterrence strategy was on full display last week
when the Iranian Parliament initiated a fast-track process to formalise into law
Tehran’s management of the Strait of Hormuz. According to one Iranian MP,
the plan has four objectives: shipping security, environmental pollution
tolls, collecting fees for navigational guidance and creating a regional
development fund for reconstruction. Underscoring this shift, the Iranian
navy earlier this week said that the Strait of Hormuz would “never return to its
former state” and vowed to impose a “new Gulf order.” And last weekend, in an
early sign of coordination between Tehran and Oman – the other coastal state
on the Strait of Hormuz – Iranian and Omani officials met to discuss “possible

WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
First, it affords Tehran enormous influence and power to effectively run a
permissioned gate-keeper toll system through the Strait of Hormuz. And here lies
a critical nuance. For the regime, a complete closure of Hormuz is not a sustainable
strategy as it would risk further isolation and would invite overwhelming
international pressure. However, a permissioned Strait of Hormuz – one that
remains open for non-combatant countries and those not allied with the
U.S./Israel – is a very different proposition. Pursuing this path, Tehran will
calculate that it has time on its side until countries faced with growing economic
pressure to either export their products (i.e. the Gulf countries) or to secure
supplies (i.e. Asia/Africa/potentially some European countries) approach Tehran
on a one-to-one basis to secure a deal and guarantee safe passage.
While vessel traffic through Hormuz is still a fraction of what it was before
February 28, a growing list of countries including India, China, Russia, Iraq
and a number of South East Asian countries have managed to secure safe
passage for some of their vessels by engaging diplomatically with Iran. This
in itself has had the unintended consequence of bolstering the international
recognition of the regime at a time when it was weakened following the
January protests and now, after six weeks of war, has become even more radical,
embittered and revengeful towards U.S./Israeli interests. With Trump’s stinging
rebukes of NATO’s non-involvement in the war coupled with Foreign Policy last
week describing U.S.-South Korea relations as being at “breaking point,” it begs
the question “who has become more isolated in the last six weeks: US/Israel, or Iran?”
An article this week in the New York Times by Robert Pape, Professor of political
science at the University of Chicago, argued how Iran’s permissioned system
over the Strait of Hormuz could elevate its relative power. In a piece titled “The
War Is Turning Iran Into a Major World Power,” Pape argued:
In recent years, the conventional geopolitical wisdom has been that the
world order was moving toward three centers of power: the United States,
China and Russia. That view assumed that power derived primarily from
economic scale and military capability.
That assumption no longer holds. A fourth center of global power is quickly
emerging — Iran — that does not rival those three nations economically or
militarily. Instead, its newfound power derives from its control over the
most important energy choke point in the global economy, the Strait of
Hormuz.

President Macron of France echoed similar comments last week when he said:
The liberation of the Strait of Hormuz by force through a military operation…
It is unrealistic…This can only be done in concert with Iran.
However, Hormuz taking a more central role in Tehran’s national security and
deterrence posture has implications beyond geopolitics. It also has global
geoeconomic implications.
Just over four years ago, in WILTW March 3, 2022, we discussed the importance
that the so-called Triple Axis of China, Russia and Iran would have on the global
order.
We wrote:
While the relationship between the three countries is nuanced, and
internal differences and suspicions do exist, they are nonetheless
bound by a common cause of achieving self-reliance, reducing their
dependence on the dollar and challenging the incumbent international
order that, from their perspective, they had no say in creating.  These
motivations will only grow with time. They will not dissipate.
We went onto quote from a book called Triple Axis: Iran’s Relations with Russia
and China, by Dina Esfandiary and Ariane Tabatabai:
The three countries seek to assert themselves as regional hegemons and
reduce the influence of the West, particularly the United States, in what
they view as their own backyards. Ultimately, Beijing, Moscow and Tehran
want the international order to better reflect the interests of “rising” non-
Western states…
Fast forward to today and Iran’s nascent efforts to formalise its leverage over
Hormuz aligns with Moscow and Beijing’s longer-term ambitions to challenge
the dollar’s monopoly on energy pricing and to create an alternative monetary,
trade and financial system that is based on gold as the reserve asset and not
U.S. Treasuries. While Iran is sanctioned out of the Western financial system,
receiving payments in CNY to allow vessels through Hormuz could further boost
Beijing’s efforts to internationalise the CNY and to financialise the currency
around gold (see reports).

Price inflation. In our view, any discussion of the effects of tariffs must focus

Inbound FDI. Although the White House frequently advertises trillions of dollars
in promises of investment from other countries, so far, foreign direct investment
(FDI) inflows have not registered any growth. In fact, inbound FDI has been in
a downward trend since 2Q-2025, and total FDI inflows of $288.4 billion
in 2025 were lower than the annual totals from 2021 to 2024, according to
Cato. (See chart below.)
This rate of FDI inflows is far short of the amount needed to achieve the
administration’s stated goal of $18 trillion of foreign investment, although this
figure has been widely criticized for being over-inflated and non-binding. And
given the current economic and geopolitical strains over the situation in Iran,
the likelihood of meaningful near-term increases in foreign investment appears
slim. With Persian Gulf sovereign wealth funds (SWFs) controlling $5 trillion
of assets, and the EU holding investments of $12.6 trillion in the U.S., the
risk of repatriation of capital flows has never been greater.

Is the administration seriously considering using nuclear weapons? The White
House response has been ambiguous, with the rapid response twitter account

Yet, shortly after, Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said “Only the President knows

potential use by Russia, despite the fact that for the reasons noted above the
most likely first user would be the United States. Until recently there was
little discussion of the ramifications of first use by the U.S. The most likely
immediate impact would be widespread international condemnation. Typically,
the international community would institute crippling sanctions and trade
restrictions, however, while the U.S. role has diminished over recent years,
the central role it still plays in the Western economic system would be hard
to replace on short notice. Nevertheless, nuclear weapon use by the
U.S. would dramatically accelerate the attempts by many
countries to disengage from America.
A major result of the use of a nuclear weapon would be a mad scramble by non-
nuclear states who have the ability to obtain them to achieve a nuclear capacity
as quickly as possible. A number of states are thought to have the capability to
rapidly build nuclear weapons, such as Japan, Germany, South Korea, and Saudi
Arabia. Other countries could likely do so within years. Essentially anyone
currently running a nuclear power plant could technically build a nuclear
weapon – and many would probably do so.

“We urgently need something to lead us out
of the shallows back into the depths, out of
the two-dimensional world of the abstract
and theoretical,
into the full-bloodedness of
embodied life.” How did we lose touch?
Iain McGilchrist has dedicated his work to understanding the discrepancy
between the left and right hemispheres of the brain, and the kinds of worlds
that emerge when their relationship becomes imbalanced. His claim is not merely
neurological, but civilizational: the crisis we face reflects a deeper shift in how
we attend to reality.
This piece marks the beginning of a four-part series examining that shift. Here,
we begin with diagnosis: how did we lose reality? In the pieces that follow in
WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
coming weeks, we will turn to the divided mind and the relationship between
mythos and logos, the rise of power without wisdom, the externalization of
left-hemisphere modes of thought into systems, and finally the possibility
of returning to depth and recovering meaning. This is not simply cultural
decline. It reflects a more fundamental transformation in how we encounter
the world.
McGilchrist begins with a deceptively simple observation: human beings learn
through experience which comes with maturation. Unlike machines, we age,
we witness change, and we remember how things can be otherwise. As he
writes:
You see more, not of course because of any innately superior faculty, but for no
better reason than that you have lived through many changes and remember
how things can be different, for better or worse, in a way which is hard to
understand unless you experienced it.
Experience, in this sense, is not incidental. It is foundational. It grounds
judgment in a way that abstract knowledge cannot. Yet, we are increasingly
distanced from it. Not only are opportunities for lived, embodied experience
eroding, but even where experience is available, it is often subordinated to theory.
In the absence of experience, or in the refusal to trust it, we turn instead to
abstraction as our guide. This inversion lies at the core of McGilchrist’s concern:
a world in which theory begins to replace life.
This tension is not new. In the Meno, Plato presents the thought experiment
known as the road to Larissa, which asks whether experience or theoretical
knowledge is the better guide to action. The contrast is clear: one may know
the route to Larissa through repeated travel or possess a correct account of it
through reasoning. Both may arrive, but only one has lived the path. What is
new is the extent to which we now favor the latter.
We are raising a generation for whom experience itself can be substituted. It is
increasingly sufficient to know about something without ever undergoing
it. One can understand heartbreak as a concept, simulate it through virtual
systems, or model its emotional contours without ever having risked attachment
or loss.

WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
But this substitution is not neutral. To know that heartbreak is painful is not
the same as having one’s world reorganized by it. The latter is not information,
it is transformation. It unfolds in time: in the first spark of connection, the slow
accumulation of trust, the quiet weaving together of two lives, and then, just as
gradually—or all at once—the rupture. It is the disorientation of absence, the
strange persistence of memory, the way a song, a street, a passing scent
can reopen what one believed had closed.
No simulation can reproduce this. It cannot replicate the investment of self, the
vulnerability of exposure, or the irreversible passage through joy into loss. These
are not variables to be modeled, but experiences that reconstitute the person
who undergoes them. This is why developmental psychologists emphasize
that learning is not merely intellectual. A child does not simply acquire
knowledge but develops through embodied interaction with the world through
movement, resistance, and failure. Abstract reasoning and higher-order
cognition are not prior to this process but emerge from it.
McGilchrist connects this erosion of experience to a broader cultural shift. Citing
Walter Benjamin, he writes:
The art of storytelling is coming to an end. It is as if something that seemed
inalienable to us, the securest among our possessions, were taken from
us: the ability to exchange experiences. One reason for this phenomenon is
obvious: experience has fallen in value. And it looks as if it is continuing to fall
into bottomlessness.
The contemporary obsession with “storytelling” only confirms this
diagnosis. Across industries, companies now compete to hire “storytellers,” as if
the capacity to give meaningful form to experience were a scarce technical skill
(See WILTW February 26, 2026). But that is precisely the point. What was once
a shared human inheritance has become something to be outsourced. The
ability to tell stories, draw from lived experience, render it intelligible, and pass
it on no longer resides within a culture, but must be artificially reintroduced
into it. That we must now hire storytellers is itself evidence that we have
forgotten how to tell stories. Author Joan Didion said it best: “We tell ourselves
stories in order to live.” The human mind needs to create narratives to extract
meaning and make sense of chaos, suffering, joy, gratitude, and so on.

WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
This is what Benjamin saw with such clarity about a century ago: as experience
diminishes, so too does the ability to communicate it. What disappears is not
simply a form of expression, but a mode of truth. This is why McGilchrist
can insist that “the art of storytelling is vital to truth.” And why its
loss leaves us not only less connected, but less able to recognize truth when it
appears.
For much of human history, myths were not regarded as falsehoods, but
as vessels of insight. We have written at length on the importance of fables
and myths, not merely for teaching children, but for highlighting what makes us
uniquely human (See WILTW July 13, 2023, May 29, 2025, June 5, 2025). Myths
carried patterns of meaning that could not be reduced to conceptual language.
As McGilchrist writes: “myths embody wisdom that cannot be conveyed in the
vapid language of concepts.”
What emerges, then, is not simply a culture that has changed its habits,
but one that has altered its way of seeing. We have not just accumulated new
tools or new technologies; we have come to inhabit a different world that is
increasingly mediated by abstraction, detached from experience, and flattened
into what can be modeled, predicted, and controlled.
This is why the problem cannot be reduced to any single domain. It is not only
that our technologies simulate experience, or that our institutions privilege
theory over practice, or that storytelling has been hollowed out. It is that the
very faculty by which we recognize reality— by which we encounter depth,
context, and meaning —has been displaced. To lose touch with reality, in this
sense, is not to step outside of it, but to begin mistaking its representations for
the thing itself. We have not lost reality all at once, but learned, slowly, to
look past it. What remains is to recover the depth we no longer know how
to see.

Nearly two decades ago, we explored the potential impact that an
electromagnetic pulse (EMP) weapon could have on Western economies (see
WILTW August 7, 2008, and other reports). Although the risk of a nuclear-based
EMP attack is low, it is an existential, high-consequence danger. Of all the possible
types of asymmetric attacks, this is the one that scares us the most.
Non-nuclear-based EMP weapons can inflict localized and regionalized
devastation of strategic targets, especially when combined with other
asymmetric weapons, whether cyber or biological-based (i.e., a compound
attack). The risk of an asymmetric attack by Iran or its proxies (or one that
is blamed on Iran) on the U.S. homeland grows more likely as the West-
Asia conflict persists (see section 1). If the U.S. elects to detonate tactical
nuclear or EMP devices in Iran, America would likely quickly become a target
for asymmetric attacks. Progress has been made in hardening America’s
infrastructure, but significant vulnerabilities remain across critical networks.

physiological danger to humans. However, it would emit gamma rays
that create Compton electrons, which move nearly at the speed of light
along the line of sight, damaging electrical and electronic systems. A
weapon detonated at an altitude of 300 miles above Chicago would send an
electromagnetic wave covering the entire country. It would also destroy a
substantial amount of the satellite infrastructure on which America is highly
dependent. The 1962 Starfish Prime test at 400 kilometers caused electrical
failures in Hawaii approximately 1,400 kilometers away.
The true magnitude of the EMP threat extends beyond immediate
destruction to the cascading consequences that follow. The electric grid
serves as the foundation for nearly all other systems—water distribution, fuel
supply, transportation, communications, healthcare, and food logistics—and
when it fails, these systems begin to break down in rapid succession. Because
modern cities rely on just-in-time supply chains and maintain only a few days’
worth of essential resources, even a short-term disruption can escalate into
widespread shortages.
If outages last weeks, months, or even years, the inability to restore power—
partly due to long replacement times for critical grid parts—could cause
major societal disruption. In this scenario, the country’s interconnected,
tech-dependent infrastructure becomes vulnerable, as power outages
halt the mechanisms sustaining daily life, risking large-scale humanitarian
consequences. In a nutshell, America’s infrastructure could theoretically
resemble that of the 1830s, which supported a population of about 30 million.
The risk of a localized or regional non-nuclear EMP attack carries higher
plausibility for tactical or facility-level strikes. Non-nuclear EMP includes
high-power microwave (HPM) weapons, intentional electromagnetic-
interference (IEMI) devices, and specialized bombs that can couple
energy into electronics without a nuclear blast. A cluster of critical assets or
infrastructure, including substations, data centers, and industrial control rooms,
can be paralyzed.
Major militaries have demonstrated and fielded non-nuclear-based EMP
weapons specifically to disable electronics over localized areas. Non-
state actors and terrorists could also potentially obtain or assemble IEMI devices
with state support or access to specialized components.

Iran is believed to have the scientific and industrial knowledge base to
pursue non-nuclear EMP weapons, regardless of whether related facilities
were damaged in the current conflict. A 2025 West Asia News Agency-
linked analysis cited an Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) associated
general claiming Iran already had “electromagnetic (EMP) missiles” capable
of disabling electronics out to roughly 200 km. A 2025 SpecialEurasia analysis
also described how Iran’s new “Rastakhiz” missile is integrating nuclear and
EMP-strike capabilities, framing EMP as part of Iran’s deterrent posture.
The U.S. government has several initiatives to mitigate risks, but
progress remains insufficient. The U.S. is hardening infrastructure against
asymmetric EMP attacks and large solar flares, which can produce similar
effects. Initiatives to modernize and harden grid infrastructure from multiple
threats, including EMPs, total over $10 billion in funding. These initiatives
include the 2019 presidential-executive order on EMP resilience, energy-
sector strategies led by the Department of Energy, and ongoing defense
and infrastructure-hardening programs across the Pentagon, Department of
Homeland Security, and utilities.
However, huge gaps remain in coverage, standards, funding, and
restoration capacity across the civilian grid and other critical sectors.
Large power transformers are a key vulnerability. Loss of numerous
transformers in a large-scale attack could disable the grid for years.
Large power transformers are also difficult to shield, and many substations
still use control houses, cables, and grounding schemes that are not designed
to withstand EMPs. This exposes digital protective relays, industrial-control
systems, and communications cables to induced surges.

West-Asia conflict is a prolonged, multifaceted strategy combining
traditional terrorist violence with unconventional methods. In the near
term, the pathway from regional conflict to U.S. homeland impact is far more
likely to take the form of coordinated hybrid interference rather than a
single, nuclear-based EMP attack. For instance, combining a non-nuclear
EMP weapon with an advanced cyberattack or physical attack on a strategic
asset would amplify the effects (see WILTW April 12, 2018).

lens of children and families may prove the rare policy weapon that hits
people on an emotional level (see WILTW January 12, 2023 and April 3, 2025).
It certainly hit these two juries, nevermind the growing cohort of grassroots
initiatives led by parents and communities to loosen technology’s grip on their
kids. Will lawmakers follow suit?
In the wake of the verdicts, Senators Marsha Blackburn, Republican of Tennessee,
and Richard Blumenthal, Democrat of Connecticut, called for legislators to pass
their bill, the Kids Online Safety Act (KOSA), which stalled out in the House last
year. If passed, federal momentum would build on laws in more than 30
states banning phones in schools.
The momentum has worldwide reach. In the wake of Australia’s passage
in December of a social-media ban for those aged 16 and under, six more
democracies are expected to pass similar bans this year, including Spain,
Norway, France, Malaysia and Indonesia.
Secondly, assuming they survive the appeals process, these cases will open
the floodgates for others to sue social-media companies using the same
winning strategy: their products were built with dangerously-habit
forming features that have led to an array of serious health problems.
Similar individual cases have already been filed around the country, and a
massive federal case with more than 2,000 plaintiffs is expected to proceed this
summer. Meta and other social-media companies are facing so much pending
litigation that keeping track of it all is nearly impossible.
The third reason these cases matter is that they have revealed a crack in
Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, which for thirty years has
been the foundation the entire internet rests on.
Section 230 is the law that says these platforms cannot be held liable for their
content, or what their users post. Now, with these two cases, juries are saying
that it’s not the content that’s the problem. It’s the entire
architecture of the platforms themselves.

This is not about, oh, “I got harmed by this particular piece of content.” This
is about the design of the whole platform. The design feels defective.
And the really crazy thing about these cases is that juries agreed with these
plaintiffs for the first time. They said, “we like this theory. We think these
products are defective.” A few years ago, we weren’t using this language. It was
just kind of this more nebulous effect of Instagram on teen girls. Now, we’re
adopting this public health framing as a way to discuss the harms of
social media. We finally have the right framing.
One legal scholar called the L.A. case in particular a “brick in the wall” that’s
been shielding Big Tech.
We quote Catherine M. Sharkey, a professor of law at New York University and an
expert on tort and product liability:
The implications of the court decisions [last month] are very, very big. We’re
in a new era, a digital era, where we have to rethink definitions for products
based on which entities might have superior information to prevent these
injuries and accidents.
In the L.A. case, the design features at issue were the filters that can make
you look “more beautiful,” along with infinite scroll, autoplay video, the
constant barrage of push notifications, and the recommendation-algorithms
that power the platform. The prosecution’s central argument was that social
media can have consequences as real as those of physical goods, and we
should think about it in the same terms. Mark Lanier, a member of K.G.M.’s
legal team, described social-media companies as “lions hunting gazelles,”
and compared their products to cigarettes, opioids, and ultra-processed foods.
But it was K.G.M.’s own account of her experiences that appeared to move
members of the jury, some of whom reportedly cried during her testimony.
The New Mexico case was really about child safety. The lawyers were arguing
that Instagram in particular has become a playground for predators, especially
via their end-to-end encrypted messaging. The thrust of the argument was that
Meta has been falsely advertising that these platforms are safe when, in
reality, children are being harmed there all the time.

New Mexico Attorney General Raúl Torrez issued a statement to reporters,
writing, “New Mexico is proud to be the first state to hold Meta accountable in
court for misleading parents, enabling child exploitation, and harming kids.”
The verdicts pose existential questions for social media companies, AI,
and the future of the internet itself. One of the big debates that’s raging in
the internet-policy community right now is, can you separate content from
design? Some argue that designing a push notification is protected under the
First Amendment. But the juries in L.A. and New Mexico called foul, essentially
saying that the container is just as dangerous as the stuff inside the container.
More juries are likely to rule the same way. Lawsuits filed by parents are
already piling up against AI chatbot companies, alleging that their products
are addictive, promote dangerous content, and have directly contributed to the
suicide and severe mental-health issues among minors—in short, the same legal
strategy that just proved successful against social media companies. In the case
of Meta, internal documents have revealed that the platform’s rules explicitly
allow their chatbots to have sensual conversations with children, meaning there
is hard evidence that Meta knowingly designed a defective and dangerous
product.
The L.A. case also raised the issue of monopoly power, with the lawyers
arguing—or at least raising the question—that in order to be truly addictive,
you have to have scale. Newton summarized it this way: “There’s something
about the fact that there’s hundreds of millions of people on Instagram creating
content that [generates] that kind of infinite supply of things that you might
want to watch that is actually able to addict you.”
And in fact, this is the tack that European regulators have taken: the bigger
and richer and more powerful you are, the more responsibility you have to
society. In this particular case, Meta has unlimited resources to hire the world’s
best cognitive scientists to figure out how to hack users’ brains with the most
addictive designs possible. The very spectre of this advantage proved persuasive
to jurors.
Newton wrote: “The jury saw that there’s no check on that kind of power
right now in U.S. society, except for this litigation.”

Finally, the arc of smartphones and social media may foretell the future for

noting: “First comes tech enthusiasm, then rapid adoption, and finally

implementing a social media ban for young people, it’s also considering
an AI-literacy plan, which focuses on embedding AI across educational
systems. If there’s a warning embedded in these trials for investors, it’s how
quickly tech hype can turn into a full-blown backlash.

noting: “First comes tech enthusiasm, then rapid adoption, and finally
disenchantment.”
Social media and smartphones are at a later stage of the cycle. Classroom AI use
is just gearing up. An illustrative example is Australia. At the very moment it’s
implementing a social media ban for young people, it’s also considering
an AI-literacy plan, which focuses on embedding AI across educational
systems. If there’s a warning embedded in these trials for investors, it’s how
quickly tech hype can turn into a full-blown backlash.
The destruction of the U.S. Air Force
F-15E Strike Eagle fighter jet last week
incinerated 0.05% of the global supply of
the critical-mineral rhenium. The war in
Iran is proving that U.S. “just-in-time”
mineral and defense-technology supply
chains are not fit-for-purpose. What are
the implications? How to invest.
Rhenium is one of the rarest elements in the earth’s crust—less than 1
part per billion—and one of the smallest metal markets worldwide. It is
primarily found in Chilean porphyry-molybdenum deposits that contain 0.001%
to 0.2% rhenium. Total global production is only 62 tons per year, excluding
approximately 15 tons from scrap and recycling. The total global supply could fit
into two pickup trucks. Global supply is mostly from Chile (29 tons), followed by
the U.S. and Poland (9.5 tons respectively), and China and Uzbekistan (~5 tons
respectively). Rhenium is genuinely rare.
Global rhenium supply is also inherently constrained given that it is only
produced as a byproduct of a byproduct. Like most niche, byproduct minor-

metals markets, increasing supply is tied to the economics of primary metal supply.
Given that rhenium does not form its own minerals and exists as a substitute
within the molybdenite lattice, its recovery is entirely dependent on the multi-
stage processing of copper-molybdenum concentrates, and it is often lost to
waste streams or flue gases. The process of extracting rhenium from copper-
molybdenum ores is a complex metallurgical challenge, making supply highly
inelastic. The rhenium market is difficult to scale.
Rhenium has recently been classified as a critical mineral by the U.S.
Geological Survey (USGS). Its inclusion signals that U.S. policymakers view it as
strategically important to economic and national security, and prone to supply-
chain disruption. Other minerals added to the 2025 USGS list were: copper,
potash, boron, lead, metallurgical coal, silicon, silver, and uranium. These
minerals were added following the USGS mapping 1,200 trade-disruption
scenarios across 84 commodities and ranking them by probability-weighted
loss to U.S. GDP from a one-year-long supply disruption.
Rhenium is not substitutable in its main downstream market: jet-engine
components. Without rhenium, there are no jet engines. Approximately 80
percent of the global market for rhenium is for nickel super-alloy turbine-
engine components—particularly the blades for commercial- and military-
jet engines. It has exceptional stability at high temperature, limiting its
substitution for performance thresholds in the hottest stages of modern
engines. In aerospace, even a small amount of rhenium can materially improve
creep resistance and oxidation control, helping engines run hotter and more
efficiently. Typical rhenium concentration in nickel-based superalloys is between
Beyond jet engines, approximately 15% of global rhenium demand is for
platinum-rhenium catalysts for petroleum reforming. Rhenium demand
in 2025 was boosted by the U.S. administration’s pro-oil stance and cuts to
renewable-energy tax-credits. Rhenium-based alloys are also increasingly used
as alternatives in medical implants.
Ultimately, the global-rhenium supply chain is yet another clear example
that the “just-in-time” defense-procurement supply chain is no longer fit
for purpose. The unvarnished truth is that Western-defense procurement relies on
small, unscalable, and highly-concentrated metals markets.

Consider the following:
The U.S. Air Force F-15E Strike Eagle fighter jet shot down over
Iran on Friday last week contained approximately 0.032 tons of
rhenium—0.05% of total annual global supply. The F-15E’s Pratt &
Whitney F100-series engines, particularly the advanced 229 variant used
in F-15 Eagle jets, use significant quantities of rhenium in their high-
pressure turbine sections. These high-pressure turbine (HPT) blades are
manufactured from the single-crystal superalloy PW1484, which typically
contains approximately 3% rhenium to enhance thermal stability and creep
resistance. Exact individual blade masses are proprietary to the company,
although industry benchmarks for F100-series engines indicate a total
rhenium loading of approximately 16.30 kg per engine, which equates to
32.60 kg for a twin-engine F-15 aircraft.
Defense aviation is manifestly the leading sector vulnerable to rhenium
supply-chain disruption. Commercial aviation is equally vulnerable. Boeing
has forecast demand of nearly 44,000 new airplanes over the next 20 years. This
increase in engine production will force significant downward pressure on
the rhenium supply chain, potentially leading to shortages. Where supply
cannot meet demand, commercial engine makers will face longer lead times
and higher inventory costs. In Western-defense-aviation supply chains, this
creates significant consequences for fleet availability and defense readiness.
Rhenium is the classic bottleneck minor-metal market. However, unlike
many other minor-metals markets we have written about, global rhenium
supply is not highly concentrated in China; nor is it particularly vulnerable
to potential Chinese-trade disruptions. Global supply is only threatened if a
copper mine, concentrator or smelter is disrupted.
Despite this, rhenium supply is still poised to become a key geopolitical-
mineral battleground of the future. Today, China is the largest global
importer of rhenium. Despite current volatile jet-fuel prices, commercial
aviation demand in China is entering a significant structural-growth phase.

Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China, Ltd. (COMAC), the largest state-
owned-aircraft manufacturer in China, drastically revised the short-term
delivery outlook of its flagship C919 aircraft due to the U.S. export ban of key
engine parts. The Aviation Industry Corporation of China (AVIC), China’s state-
owned aerospace and defense conglomerate and the primary manufacturer
of China’s domestic-military aircraft, is also a key supplier of parts for COMAC.
China’s three-largest commercial airlines are also majority state-owned.
The rhenium narrative symbolizes a wider breakdown in modern
Western-defense-procurement supply chains. As we are witnessing in
the Middle East, modern conflict is punishing these slow, outdated supply
chains. Modern warfare is increasingly shaped by inexpensive drones and
low-cost, high-return operations capable of inflicting significant equipment
losses. In the first week of the war in Iran, a single Iranian drone (costing
approximately $30,000) damaged an AN/FPS-132 phased-array early warning
radar in Qatar. The AN/FPF-132 took eight years to build and cost $1.1 billion.
The “just-in-time” procurement paradigm, whether for mining the upstream
critical minerals themselves, or for the finished components supplied by sub-
tier manufacturers, is clearly no longer effectively serving this kind of warfare
(see WILTW report).
Long-term strategic planning could address some of these endemic
supply-chain vulnerabilities. However, the Trump administration’s
downgrading of long-term strategic institutions—such as abolishing
the Department of War’s Office of Net Assessment (ONA), cutting 50% of
Office of Director of National Intelligence (ODNI) staffing, defunding the
State Department’s Global Engagement Center, and closing nearly 30 U.S.
consulates—signals the beginnings of an erosion of U.S.-strategic foresight
that will in time undermine the development of critical national industries
and their supply chains.
Rhenium is therefore more than a niche industrial input: it is part of
the resilience of an entire system. Rhenium is a single-point-of-failure in a
wider matrix of other single-points-of-failure. The real cost of the Iran war is
therefore not in dollars, or even materiel, but the weeks or months it will
take to repair and replace U.S.-defense supplies. Time is the real cost. Building
institutional capacity on this scale—repairing and restructuring the supply
chain to meet the new demands of modern warfare and address the specific
strategic-material chokepoints—will take the U.S. years it likely doesn’t have.

LNG importers face a similar challenge. For every $1-per-million-British-
thermal-units (MMBtu) increase in LNG prices, global net import costs rise
by about $20 billion per year. Goldman Sachs forecasts a $12 to $14 MMBtu
price increase for European and Asian gas imports through Q2.
For importers of oil and gas, the fastest and most affordable way to protect
themselves from the price and supply volatility of oil and gas imports is with
electrotech—solar and wind power generation, battery storage, electric
vehicles, and heat pumps, as we have previously noted. (See reports.)
Electrotech technologies could replace up to 70% of current fossil fuel
imports, according to the Ember report.
Fortunately, electrotech alternatives have become cheaper, better, and more
readily available since the 2022 energy crisis.
For example, since 2022, the cost of solar panels has dropped by half, and
annual solar installations have nearly tripled. Battery prices decreased by
36%, and yearly deployment of grid batteries increased sevenfold.
Unlike fossil fuels, wind and solar energy resources are abundant and accessible
everywhere. The sun and wind are not affected by geopolitics, tariffs,
shipping delays, chokepoints, price volatility, currency fluctuations, or
conflicts.
Although intermittent, solar and wind power have a significant efficiency
advantage since 60% to 65% of energy produced by fossil fuels is lost as waste
heat.
The capital costs of building solar and wind installations are now similar to
those of a new gas-power plant with comparable power output.

WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
We quote Sara Jane Ahmed, Managing Director and Finance Advisor of the
74-nation coalition:
The old trade-off between climate and development is over. Vulnerable
countries are leading a rapid clean energy shift, leapfrogging fossil fuels and
even richer nations.
For example, Cuba, currently under an oil embargo by the U.S., increased its
solar capacity by 350% since 2024, thanks to low-cost Chinese solar panels.
Solar and other renewables may provide 15% of Cuba’s power this year, a vital
lifeline for a country experiencing widespread blackouts due to the embargo.
Cuba plans to nearly double solar capacity by 2028.
Asian countries with coal reserves will replace some gas imports with coal.
However, it is likely to be a short-term substitution. Low-cost solar panels and
today’s much lower-cost batteries are price-competitive with existing coal
in many markets. Moreover, many countries, including Thailand, the Philippines,
South Korea, and India, have removed many permitting requirements to speed
the installation of renewables.
For example, solar and batteries can already meet 90% of India’s electricity
demand at a price competitive with existing coal, according to a new report.
Equally important, solar and batteries can provide that power without the
billions of dollars in health impacts, lost productivity, and crop losses from coal’s
air pollution.
Over 500 million people still lack access to electricity. Another 500 million face
unreliable supply. Low-cost, off-grid solar and electrification can finally bring
reliable modern energy to these people. Fossil energy is no longer essential
to economic development, the Ember report concludes.
Electric 2- and 3-wheel vehicles, along with electric cars and trucks, cut oil
consumption by 2.3 million barrels per day last year. That will jump to 5.25

Switzerland’s Department of Defence, Civil Protection and Sport (DDPS) said that
the U.S.’s “reprioritization” of deliveries “significantly alters the contractual
basis” of the acquisition. The nation’s Federal Council also added that these
changes in U.S. priorities have “profoundly altered the contractual basis.”

a contract dispute over price increases with the U.S. government.

can be suspended and, if it falls further, terminated…This could affect not
only the Patriot procurement but the entire Swiss Foreign Military Sales

materiel to countries involved in the international armed conflict with Iran
cannot be authorised for the duration of the conflict.” Furthermore, a number of
European counties have blocked airspace to the U.S. military.
The ideological rift between the U.S. and Europe continues to widen.
In a sharp rebuke of Trump’s actions, the European Council President António
Cost says: “Any targeting of civilian infrastructure, namely energy facilities, is
illegal and unacceptable.”

considering pulling the U.S. out of NATO, with the matter “beyond
reconsideration.”

In an interview with France Inter radio, Marine Le Pen of the National Rally party,
the most popular party in France, pointed to the “catastrophic consequence”
of the war in Iran, calling it a “mistake” and criticised Trump’s “erratic war goals.”

Trump’s attack on Iran “a catastrophe.”
Chrupalla has said that it is time to remove allied troops and nuclear
weapons from Germany in order to pursue an “independent” foreign policy.
At the start of the year, we noted that in just the past three years,  European
investors increased their holdings of U.S. assets by 91%, equivalent to $4.9
trillion. Furthermore, half of the $10.4 trillion in U.S. stocks held by Europe
is owned by the eight countries that Trump has threatened with tariffs. (See
WILTW January 29, 2026.)

flowing from Europe to the U.S.? Is a reversal in the cards?

East, European equity markets experienced their largest ever recorded
monthly inflow of capital.
As Jean Monnet, the Founding Father of the EU, said: “Europe will be forged in crises,
and will be the sum of the solutions adopted for those crises.”

Could Europe use this crisis to rejuvenate the single market and attract
capital?
The current crisis gives Europe added impetus to strengthen the single market
and carry out reforms to make the economy more competitive. As we see in
the following chart, as of the end of last year, the majority of Draghi’s proposals
were still in progress.

| Category | Implemented (%) | Partially Implemented (%) | In Progress (%) | Not Implemented (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Defence | 0 | 14 | 79 | 10 |
| Energy | 0 | 17 | 75 | 8 |
| Automotive | 0 | 46 | 25 | 29 |
| Clean technologies | 3 | 31 | 41 | 26 |
| Critical raw materials | 33 | 1 | 37 | 26 |
| Digitalisation and advanced technologies | 4 | 19 | 62 | 15 |
| Energy-intensive industries | 17 | 24 | 38 | 21 |
| Pharma | 0 | 28 | 56 | 17 |
| Space | 0 | 28 | 44 | 28 |
| Transport | 27 | 20 | 34 | 20 |
Source: Draghi Observatory & Implementation Index
As we wrote in WILTW November 20, 2025, creating a “military Schengen”
and strengthening military capability is a way to revive the single market. A
consequence of this is capital being repatriated back to Europe. (See WILTW
January 8, 2026.)
Last summer, Jeff Currie wrote a paper entitled “The New Martial Plan” in
which he noted the following: “Europe could spend an additional €4-5 trillion
on defence over the next decade based on current goals, rising to €9 trillion if
adjacent infrastructure is included, and at the limit could approach €14 trillion
if Europe matched what the US spent on average over the past 60 years and
what Poland is spending now.”
Europe has the fiscal space to do this. The debt-to-GDP ratio in the European Union
is 82.1% and the continent is a triple-A rated entity, an increasing rarity.

“Because friends may come and go, friendships are a context in which teens
WHAT I LEARNED THIS WEEK
April 9, 2026
must develop skills to maintain and grow the friendship or risk its loss. These
skills are likely to be subsequently helpful for forming future friendships and
longer-term romantic relationships.”
As we’ve written about extensively in recent years, rates of loneliness have
soared as technology—led in no small part by the advent of the smartphone in
2007—has increasingly dominated how we live, work, and socialize (see articles).
The cost of this shift is enormous: Loneliness makes people more prone to
depression, dementia progression, hypertension, stroke, and cancer.
But Big Tech isn’t the sole culprit of this development. The dominance of a
screen-based life and the rise of social media merely accelerated a social
isolation trend that began long before the first personal computer hit the
market. In his seminal 2000 book,  Bowling Alone: The Collapse and Revival of
American Community, political scientist Robert D. Putnam examined this change.
Putnam argues that the 1970s marked a new era of withdrawal in American
life as the shared interest and investment in social infrastructure—our
community centers, our libraries, our public pools—waned. Involvement
in a range of organizations, from churches to book clubs to bowling leagues,
eroded. From 1985 to 1994 alone, participation in these kinds of community
endeavors fell by nearly half.
In retreating from community, Americans have also retreated from each
other. One landmark study found that the percentage of U.S. adults who
report having zero close friends has quadrupled since 1990 to 12%, while
the number of those who report having ten or more close friends has fallen
to one-third of its previous level.
The writer and podcaster Derek Thompson describes this movement as the
“privatization of leisure.” He argues that “America is suffering a kind of ritual
recession, with fewer community-based routines and more entertainment for,
and empowerment of, individuals and the aloneness that they choose.”
The data on young people is especially telling. In 1996, American high school
sophomores went out with friends an average of 2.5 times per week, according
to the long-running Monitoring the Future project at the University of Michigan.
A decade later, at the very time when AOL Instant Messenger had gone
mainstream, that same age bracket was only hanging out in person 2.3 times
per week. By 2020—just before the pandemic roiled social life— the rate had
collapsed to 1.5 times per week.

As we’ve written about extensively in recent years, rates of loneliness have
soared as technology—led in no small part by the advent of the smartphone in
2007—has increasingly dominated how we live, work, and socialize (see articles).
The cost of this shift is enormous: Loneliness makes people more prone to
depression, dementia progression, hypertension, stroke, and cancer.
But Big Tech isn’t the sole culprit of this development. The dominance of a
screen-based life and the rise of social media merely accelerated a social
isolation trend that began long before the first personal computer hit the
market. In his seminal 2000 book,  Bowling Alone: The Collapse and Revival of
American Community, political scientist Robert D. Putnam examined this change.
Putnam argues that the 1970s marked a new era of withdrawal in American
life as the shared interest and investment in social infrastructure—our
community centers, our libraries, our public pools—waned. Involvement
in a range of organizations, from churches to book clubs to bowling leagues,
eroded. From 1985 to 1994 alone, participation in these kinds of community
endeavors fell by nearly half.
In retreating from community, Americans have also retreated from each
other. One landmark study found that the percentage of U.S. adults who
report having zero close friends has quadrupled since 1990 to 12%, while
the number of those who report having ten or more close friends has fallen
to one-third of its previous level.
The writer and podcaster Derek Thompson describes this movement as the
“privatization of leisure.” He argues that “America is suffering a kind of ritual
recession, with fewer community-based routines and more entertainment for,
and empowerment of, individuals and the aloneness that they choose.”
The data on young people is especially telling. In 1996, American high school
sophomores went out with friends an average of 2.5 times per week, according
to the long-running Monitoring the Future project at the University of Michigan.
A decade later, at the very time when AOL Instant Messenger had gone
mainstream, that same age bracket was only hanging out in person 2.3 times
per week. By 2020—just before the pandemic roiled social life— the rate had
collapsed to 1.5 times per week.

All manner of teen life has paid the price: Young Americans are less likely than
previous generations to get their driver’s license, to go on a date, to get a
job. In the late 1970s, 77 percent of high-school seniors had part-time work
during the school year; by the mid-2010s, only 55 percent did.
One of the leading experts in this field of study is Dr. Joseph Allen, a professor
of psychology at the University of Virginia. Since 1998, he and his team have
overseen a longitudinal study of nearly 200 participants who were 13 years old
at the time of the project’s launch.
In an important 2021 survey Allen’s team asked study participants, who were
at the time 27 years old, questions about the quality of their adult social and
romantic relationships, as well as about their depressive symptoms and work
performance. Next, they compared the data to earlier interviews with the cohort,
their parents, and teen friends on relationship quality. Good teen friendships,
the team found, fostered success later in life.
“Quality relationships with teens were a far better predictor of all of these
outcomes than was the quality of the teens’ relationships with their parents,”
Allen reported. “The flip side is just as you’d expect, which is if you did not have
good, quality, best-friend relationships as a teenager, in adulthood, by age 25, 26
and 27, you had worse work performance, you had lower-quality relationships
with other adults, you had poor-quality romantic relationships and you were
more likely to be depressed.”
All of this comes at an interesting point in human history. Despite all of our
aloneness, American life has never felt so crowded. Our days are consumed
by communication: texting, posting, DMing, emailing, calling. This new
order of relationship maintenance is unending and exhausting.
Has modern life left us too tired to do the hard work of forming real friendships?
Nurturing real bonds with others requires time and patience. It means navigating
discomforts, permitting vulnerabilities, and allowing for self-reflection and even
criticism. Our increasingly-dominant online life not only consumes time for
real-world relationships but also disincentivizes our pursuit of them.

physical pain,” writes Carolyn Bruckmann in the February 2025 issue of Harvard
Kennedy School Magazine. “Over time, this makes socializing feel riskier, and to
avoid that discomfort, we increasingly turn to psychological shortcuts. Digital
interactions offer a sense of control: behind our screens, we can curate our
self-presentation, sidestep awkward social cues, and get quick dopamine
hits that create the illusion of connection. Given the way our brains are wired,
we also gravitate toward the safest, most familiar spaces—choosing the comfort
of home and close family over the unpredictability of community spaces and
new relationships.”
Still, humans are inherently social animals. It is in our lifeblood to make and
build connections. To feel connected. And as we’ve noted previously, there is
a growing hunger from younger generations to forge a healthy life outside of
tech’s domain (see WILTW May 22, 2025; February 12, 2026). Across the country,
several innovative programs have emerged to help young Americans
overcome the country’s friendship recession.
Here are a few that have caught our attention:
Project for Public Spaces (PPS): Working with different local organizations,
PPS transforms “dead” urban spaces into community gathering spots. In San
Antonio, Texas, for example, it helped turn a concrete underpass into
a dedicated teen hangout, complete with a shaded basketball court,
ping pong tables, large picnic tables, and seating areas that encourage
“loitering.”
Log Off Movement: Launched in 2020 by a high school senior in response to
her own struggles with social media, Log Off provides a toolkit for teens to
build analog networks in their own towns. Its popular “Forks Up, Phones
Down” campaign encourages teens to dine together to build authentic
human connection.
CoGenerate: This national program matches teens with seniors to work
together on projects that address different issues affecting their
respective communities, from climate action to healthcare access.
These programs are an inspiration and we hope in the coming years to see
others like them develop. The vitality of our survival depends on it.
